questions.text: Which of the following is not a part of an atom?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Photon**

**Explanation:**
An atom consists of three fundamental subatomic particles:
• **Electron** – Negatively charged particle
• **Proton** – Positively charged particle
• **Neutron** – Neutral particle

A **photon** is not a component of the atom. It is a quantum of electromagnetic radiation (light particle).

**Option Analysis:**
Electron – Fundamental particle discovered by J.J. Thomson.
Proton – Positive particle discovered through Rutherford’s experiments.
Neutron – Neutral particle discovered by James Chadwick in 1932.
Photon – Particle of light, not part of atomic structure.

**Exam Insight:**
Questions may also ask:
• Charge of electron = −1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C
• Mass of electron ≈ 1/1836 of proton.

questions.text: A single type of atom is found in
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Native elements**

**Explanation:**
Native elements exist in nature in a **pure elemental form** consisting of only one type of atom.

Examples:
• Gold (Au)
• Silver (Ag)
• Copper (Cu)

**Option Analysis:**
Compounds of minerals – Contain two or more elements chemically combined.
Mixture of minerals – Physical mixture of substances.
Native elements – Only one type of atom present.

**Exam Insight:**
Native elements are often found in metallic form in Earth's crust.

questions.text: Constituents of atomic nucleus are
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Proton and neutron**

**Explanation:**
The nucleus of an atom contains:
• **Protons** (positively charged)
• **Neutrons** (neutral)

Electrons revolve around the nucleus in energy levels.

**Exam Insight:**
Mass number = Protons + Neutrons
Atomic number = Number of protons.

questions.text: Which of the following statement about molecular structure is correct?
options: Neutron and electron are found inside the nucleus and protons revolves around the nucleus
Electron and proton are inside the nucleus and neutron revolve around the nucleus
Proton and neutron are inside the nucleus and electrons revolve around the nucleus
Proton, neutron and electron all are inside the nucleus
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Proton and neutron are inside the nucleus and electrons revolve around the nucleus**

**Explanation:**
According to the modern atomic model:
• The **nucleus contains protons and neutrons**.
• **Electrons revolve around the nucleus** in orbitals or shells.

This structure was explained through Rutherford's model and later refined by Bohr and quantum mechanics.

questions.text: The atomic nucleus was discovered by
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Rutherford**

**Explanation:**
Ernest Rutherford discovered the atomic nucleus through the **Gold Foil Experiment (1911)**.

Observation:
Most alpha particles passed through foil but some were deflected.

Conclusion:
• Atom mostly empty space
• Positive charge concentrated in nucleus.

questions.text: In atom, neutron was discovered by
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Chadwick**

**Explanation:**
James Chadwick discovered the neutron in **1932**.

Importance:
• Explained isotopes
• Completed atomic structure model.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a subatomic particle?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Deuteron**

**Explanation:**
Deuteron is not a fundamental particle. It is the **nucleus of deuterium (heavy hydrogen)**.

Composition of deuteron:
• 1 proton
• 1 neutron.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs constitutes particle-antiparticle pair?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Electron-Positron**

**Explanation:**
A positron is the **antiparticle of an electron**.

Properties:
• Same mass
• Opposite charge.

questions.text: The alpha particle carries two positive charges. Its mass is very nearly equal to that of
options: Sum of masses of two positrons and two neutrons
Two positrons are each positron carries a single positive charge
questions.solution:
This is a **basic nuclear physics question** often asked in the General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**The alpha particle carries two positive charges. Its mass is very nearly equal to that of**

A. Two protons
B. An atom of helium
C. Sum of masses of two positrons and two neutrons
D. Two positrons

✅ **Correct Answer: B. An atom of helium**

---

# Core Concept

An **alpha particle** is actually the **nucleus of a helium atom**.

Composition of an alpha particle:

[
2 \text{ protons} + 2 \text{ neutrons}
]

Therefore:

• Charge = **+2** (because of two protons)
• Mass ≈ **4 atomic mass units (u)**

Symbolically:

[
\alpha = ^4_2He^{2+}
]

So its mass is **almost equal to the mass of a helium atom**, except that a helium atom normally also contains **two electrons**, whose mass is extremely small.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Two protons ❌ (Incorrect)

Mass of **two protons ≈ 2 u**.

But an alpha particle contains:

• **2 protons**
• **2 neutrons**

Total mass ≈ **4 u**.

So the mass is **not equal to two protons**.

---

## B. An atom of helium ✔ (Correct)

A helium atom contains:

• 2 protons
• 2 neutrons
• 2 electrons

An alpha particle is simply the **helium nucleus without electrons**.

Since electron mass is extremely small, the **mass of an alpha particle is nearly equal to the mass of a helium atom**.

Thus this option is **correct**.

---

## C. Sum of masses of two positrons and two neutrons ❌ (Incorrect)

A **positron** is the antimatter counterpart of an electron.

An alpha particle **does not contain positrons**.

It contains:

• **Protons**
• **Neutrons**

Thus this statement is incorrect.

---

## D. Two positrons ❌ (Incorrect)

A positron carries **+1 charge**, but alpha particles are **not composed of positrons**.

Also, the mass of two positrons would be **extremely small compared to the mass of an alpha particle**.

Therefore this option is incorrect.

---

# Key Facts to Remember for Exams

| Particle | Composition | Charge |
|---|---|---|
| Alpha particle | 2 protons + 2 neutrons | +2 |
| Beta particle | Electron or positron | −1 or +1 |
| Gamma ray | Electromagnetic radiation | 0 |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember:

**Alpha particle = Helium nucleus**

[
\alpha = ^4_2He^{2+}
]

So its mass is approximately **equal to the mass of a helium atom**.

---

You can also practice **important nuclear physics MCQs on alpha, beta, gamma radiation, radioactivity, and half-life**, which frequently appear in **DRDO and SSC examinations**. Below are **high-probability nuclear physics facts and MCQs** commonly asked in the General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**. These usually test **basic properties of α, β, γ radiation and radioactivity**.

---

# 1. Alpha (α) Particles

**Nature:** Helium nucleus

Composition:

[
2 \text{ protons} + 2 \text{ neutrons}
]

Symbol:

[
^4_2He^{2+}
]

Properties:

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Charge | +2 |
| Mass | ≈ 4 amu |
| Penetration power | Low |
| Ionizing power | Very high |

Example source: **Ernest Rutherford experiments on radioactivity.**

---

# 2. Beta (β) Particles

**Nature:** High-speed electrons or positrons.

Types:

• **β⁻ (electron)**
• **β⁺ (positron)**

Properties:

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Charge | −1 or +1 |
| Mass | Very small |
| Penetration | Medium |
| Ionization | Moderate |

---

# 3. Gamma (γ) Rays

**Nature:** Electromagnetic radiation.

Properties:

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Charge | 0 |
| Mass | 0 |
| Penetration | Very high |
| Ionization | Low |

Gamma rays are similar to **X-rays but more energetic**.

---

# Comparison of α, β, γ Radiation

| Radiation | Nature | Charge | Penetration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Alpha | Helium nucleus | +2 | Low |
| Beta | Electron / positron | −1 / +1 | Medium |
| Gamma | Electromagnetic radiation | 0 | Very high |

---

# Important Shielding (Very Common Question)

| Radiation | Stopped By |
|---|---|
| Alpha | Paper or skin |
| Beta | Aluminium sheet |
| Gamma | Thick lead or concrete |

---

# Important Radioactivity Facts

1. **Radioactivity** was discovered by **Henri Becquerel** in 1896.
2. **Radium and polonium** were discovered by **Marie Curie**.
3. Radioactive decay follows the **half-life law**.

---

# Half-Life

**Half-life** is the time required for **half of the radioactive substance to decay**.

Example:

If half-life = **10 years**

| Time | Remaining material |
|---|---|
| 0 years | 100% |
| 10 years | 50% |
| 20 years | 25% |
| 30 years | 12.5% |

---

# Example Exam MCQs

### 1. Which radiation has the highest penetrating power?

A. Alpha
B. Beta
C. Gamma
D. Proton

✅ **Answer: Gamma**

---

### 2. Alpha particles are stopped by:

A. Paper
B. Aluminium
C. Lead
D. Concrete

✅ **Answer: Paper**

---

### 3. Beta particles are:

A. Helium nuclei
B. Electrons
C. Neutrons
D. Photons

✅ **Answer: Electrons**

---

### 4. Radioactivity was discovered by:

A. Rutherford
B. Bohr
C. Becquerel
D. Thomson

✅ **Answer: Becquerel**

---

# 10-Second Exam Memory Trick

Remember this order:

**Penetration power**

[
\alpha < \beta < \gamma
]

**Ionizing power**

[
\gamma < \beta < \alpha
]

---

You can also review **important one-line Physics facts (modern physics, electricity, optics)** which frequently appear in **DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1 examinations**.

questions.text: The Nucleus of Helium has
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Two protons and two neutrons**

Helium nucleus contains **2 protons + 2 neutrons**.

Mass number = 4.

questions.text: Which of the following carries a negative charge?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Beta particles**

Beta particles are **high-speed electrons emitted from radioactive decay**.

questions.text: In an atom, the order of filling up of the orbitals is governed by
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Aufbau’s principle**

Electrons fill orbitals in **increasing energy order**.

questions.text: Regarding the atom of a chemical element, the magnetic quantum number refers to
questions.solution:
This question is from **atomic structure and quantum numbers**, a topic sometimes asked in the Chemistry/Physics portion of **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Regarding the atom of a chemical element, the magnetic quantum number refers to**

A. Orientation
B. Shape
C. Size
D. Spin

✅ **Correct Answer: A. Orientation**

---

# Core Concept: Quantum Numbers

Electrons in an atom are described using **four quantum numbers**.
Each quantum number gives specific information about the electron.

| Quantum Number | Symbol | What it Describes |
|---|---|---|
| Principal quantum number | (n) | Size/energy level of orbital |
| Azimuthal quantum number | (l) | Shape of orbital |
| Magnetic quantum number | (m_l) | Orientation of orbital |
| Spin quantum number | (m_s) | Spin of electron |

---

# Magnetic Quantum Number (m_l)

The **magnetic quantum number** describes the **orientation of an orbital in space** relative to a magnetic field.

Possible values:

[
 m_l = -l \text{ to } +l
]

Example:

If (l = 1) (p orbital):

[
 m_l = -1, 0, +1
]

This corresponds to the **three orientations of p orbitals**:

• (p_x)
• (p_y)
• (p_z)

Thus the magnetic quantum number determines **how an orbital is oriented in space**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Orientation ✔ (Correct)

Magnetic quantum number determines the **orientation of orbitals** in space.

Example:

p orbitals can be oriented along **x, y, or z axes**.

Therefore this option is **correct**.

---

## B. Shape ❌ (Incorrect)

The **shape of the orbital** is determined by the **azimuthal quantum number (l)**.

Examples:

| (l) value | Orbital | Shape |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | s | Spherical |
| 1 | p | Dumbbell |
| 2 | d | Cloverleaf |

Thus shape is **not determined by the magnetic quantum number**.

---

## C. Size ❌ (Incorrect)

The **size of an orbital** is determined by the **principal quantum number (n)**.

Example:

Higher (n) → larger orbital size.

Therefore this option is incorrect.

---

## D. Spin ❌ (Incorrect)

The **spin quantum number (m_s)** determines the **direction of electron spin**.

Possible values:

[
 m_s = +\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}
]

So spin is **not related to the magnetic quantum number**.

---

# Quick Summary

| Quantum Number | Symbol | Describes |
|---|---|---|
| Principal | (n) | Size / energy level |
| Azimuthal | (l) | Shape |
| Magnetic | (m_l) | Orientation |
| Spin | (m_s) | Electron spin |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember the order:

**n → size**
**l → shape**
**m_l → orientation**
**m_s → spin**

Therefore the correct answer is:

✅ **Orientation**.

questions.text: What is the mass number of an element, the atom of which contains two protons, two neutrons and two electrons?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: 4**

Mass number = Protons + Neutrons = 2 + 2 = 4.

questions.text: The atoms, in which the number of protons is same but the number of neutrons is different, are known as
questions.solution:
This question is from **atomic structure**, a common topic in Chemistry for **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**The atoms, in which the number of protons is same but the number of neutrons is different, are known as**

A. Isobars
B. Isomers
C. Isotones
D. Isotopes

✅ **Correct Answer: D. Isotopes**

---

# Core Concept

Atoms are characterized by:

• **Protons (atomic number (Z))**
• **Neutrons**
• **Mass number (A)**

[
A = \text{protons} + \text{neutrons}
]

If **two atoms have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons**, they are called **isotopes**.

Example of isotopes of **Hydrogen**:

| Isotope | Protons | Neutrons |
|---|---|---|
| Protium | 1 | 0 |
| Deuterium | 1 | 1 |
| Tritium | 1 | 2 |

All have **1 proton** but different neutrons → **isotopes**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Isobars ❌ (Incorrect)

**Isobars** are atoms of **different elements** having the **same mass number** but different atomic numbers.

Example:

| Element | Atomic Number | Mass Number |
|---|---|---|
| Argon | 18 | 40 |
| Calcium | 20 | 40 |

They have the **same mass number (40)** but belong to different elements.

Thus this option is incorrect.

---

## B. Isomers ❌ (Incorrect)

**Isomers** are compounds that have:

• Same **molecular formula**
• Different **structural arrangement**

Example:

| Compound | Formula |
|---|---|
| Ethanol | C₂H₆O |
| Dimethyl ether | C₂H₆O |

These are **chemical compounds**, not atoms.

Thus this option is incorrect.

---

## C. Isotones ❌ (Incorrect)

**Isotones** are atoms that have the **same number of neutrons but different numbers of protons**.

Example:

| Element | Protons | Neutrons |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon-14 | 6 | 8 |
| Nitrogen-15 | 7 | 8 |

Both have **8 neutrons**, so they are isotones.

Thus this option is incorrect.

---

## D. Isotopes ✔ (Correct)

**Isotopes** are atoms of the **same element** with:

• Same **atomic number (protons)**
• Different **numbers of neutrons**

Example isotopes:

• Hydrogen → ¹H, ²H, ³H
• Carbon → ¹²C, ¹³C, ¹⁴C

Therefore the correct answer is **isotopes**.

---

# Quick Comparison (Very Important for Exams)

| Term | Same | Different |
|---|---|---|
| Isotopes | Protons | Neutrons |
| Isobars | Mass number | Atomic number |
| Isotones | Neutrons | Protons |

---

# 10-Second Memory Trick

Remember the pattern:

• **IsoTOPES → same TOP (atomic number / protons)**
• **IsoTONES → same neuTRONS**
• **IsoBARS → same mass number**

---

You can also review **important Chemistry comparison terms such as isotopes, allotropes, isomers, isobars, and isotones**, which frequently appear in **DRDO, SSC, and banking examinations**. These terms often confuse students in competitive exams like **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1** because their names look similar. Examiners frequently ask **definition or identification MCQs** from them.

Below is a **clear comparison of the most important chemistry terms**.

---

# 1. Isotopes

**Definition:**
Atoms of the **same element** having the **same number of protons (atomic number)** but **different numbers of neutrons**.

Example for **Hydrogen**:

| Isotope | Protons | Neutrons |
|---|---|---|
| Protium (¹H) | 1 | 0 |
| Deuterium (²H) | 1 | 1 |
| Tritium (³H) | 1 | 2 |

Key idea:
Same element → different mass.

---

# 2. Isobars

**Definition:**
Atoms of **different elements** having the **same mass number** but different atomic numbers.

Example:

| Element | Atomic Number | Mass Number |
|---|---|---|
| Argon | 18 | 40 |
| Calcium | 20 | 40 |

Both have **mass number 40**, so they are **isobars**.

---

# 3. Isotones

**Definition:**
Atoms having the **same number of neutrons** but **different numbers of protons**.

Example:

| Element | Protons | Neutrons |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon-14 | 6 | 8 |
| Nitrogen-15 | 7 | 8 |

Both have **8 neutrons**, so they are **isotones**.

---

# 4. Isomers

**Definition:**
Compounds having the **same molecular formula but different structures**.

Example:

| Compound | Formula |
|---|---|
| Ethanol | C₂H₆O |
| Dimethyl ether | C₂H₆O |

Different structure → **isomers**.

---

# 5. Allotropes

**Definition:**
Different structural forms of the **same element in the same physical state**.

Example for **Carbon**:

| Allotrope | Property |
|---|---|
| Diamond | Hardest natural substance |
| Graphite | Soft, used in pencils |
| Fullerene | Cage-like structure |

---

# Quick Comparison Table

| Term | Same | Different |
|---|---|---|
| Isotopes | Atomic number | Neutrons |
| Isobars | Mass number | Atomic number |
| Isotones | Neutrons | Protons |
| Isomers | Molecular formula | Structure |
| Allotropes | Element | Structure |

---

# 10-Second Memory Trick

Remember the key letters:

• **IsoTOPES → same TOP (protons)**
• **IsoTONES → same neuTRONS**
• **IsoBARS → same mass numBER**

---

# Example Exam MCQ

**Atoms with the same number of neutrons but different atomic numbers are called:**

A. Isotopes
B. Isobars
C. Isotones
D. Isomers

✅ **Answer: Isotones**

---

You can also review **important “trap MCQs” from atomic structure (quantum numbers, isotopes, radioactivity)** which frequently appear in **DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1 examinations**.

questions.text: Radioactivity was discovered by
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Becquerel**

He discovered radioactivity in uranium salts in **1896**.

questions.text: Isotopes are those atoms of the same element which have

Atomic mass is different but atomic number is same
Atomic number and atoms mass both are same
questions.solution: **Correct Answer: Atomic mass different but atomic number same**

Number of protons is same but number of neutrons is different
Number of both protons and neutrons is same

questions.solution: **Correct Answer: Number of protons same but neutrons different**

questions.text: The isoneutronic group, in the following is
questions.solution:
This question tests the concept of **isotones (isoneutronic atoms)**, which often appears in the Chemistry section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**The isoneutronic group in the following is**

A. (6C^{12}, 7N^{14}, 8O^{16})
B. (6C^{12}, 7N^{14}, 8O^{18})
C. (6C^{14}, 7N^{14}, 8O^{16})
D. (6C^{14}, 7N^{15}, 8O^{16})

---

# Core Concept: Isoneutronic / Isotones

Atoms are **isotones (isoneutronic)** when they have the **same number of neutrons**.

Formula:

[
\text{Number of neutrons} = \text{Mass number} - \text{Atomic number}
]

Where:

• Atomic number = number of **protons**
• Mass number = **protons + neutrons**

---

# Check Each Option

## Option A

(6C^{12}, 7N^{14}, 8O^{16})

Calculate neutrons:

• Carbon: (12 − 6 = 6)
• Nitrogen: (14 − 7 = 7)
• Oxygen: (16 − 8 = 8)

Neutrons are **different**, so **not isotones**.

❌ Incorrect

---

## Option B

(6C^{12}, 7N^{14}, 8O^{18})

Neutrons:

• Carbon: (12 − 6 = 6)
• Nitrogen: (14 − 7 = 7)
• Oxygen: (18 − 8 = 10)

Neutrons are **different**.

❌ Incorrect

---

## Option C

(6C^{14}, 7N^{14}, 8O^{16})

Neutrons:

• Carbon: (14 − 6 = 8)
• Nitrogen: (14 − 7 = 7)
• Oxygen: (16 − 8 = 8)

Not all have the **same number of neutrons**.

❌ Incorrect

---

## Option D

(6C^{14}, 7N^{15}, 8O^{16})

Neutrons:

• Carbon: (14 − 6 = 8)
• Nitrogen: (15 − 7 = 8)
• Oxygen: (16 − 8 = 8)

All have **8 neutrons**.

✅ **Correct Answer: D**

---

# Quick Table

| Atom | Mass Number | Atomic Number | Neutrons |
|---|---|---|---|
| C-14 | 14 | 6 | 8 |
| N-15 | 15 | 7 | 8 |
| O-16 | 16 | 8 | 8 |

All have **8 neutrons → Isoneutronic group**.

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

For **isotones / isoneutronic atoms**:

[
\text{Neutrons} = A - Z
]

Check if **all neutron numbers are equal**.

---

You can also review **fast tricks to solve isotope, isobar, and isotone questions quickly**, which helps improve **time management in DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1 exams**.

questions.text: Radioactivity is measured by
questions.solution:
This is a **basic radioactivity measurement question** from Physics, commonly asked in the General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Radioactivity is measured by**

A. Hydrometer
B. Geiger Counter
C. Seismometer
D. Ammeter

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Geiger Counter**

---

# Core Concept

Radioactive substances emit **α (alpha), β (beta), and γ (gamma) radiations** during nuclear decay.

To detect and measure this radiation, scientists use an instrument called the **Geiger–Müller counter**.

It works by detecting **ionizing radiation** and converting it into **electrical pulses**, which are counted to measure radiation intensity.

---

# How a Geiger Counter Works (Simple Idea)

Main parts:

1. **Gas-filled tube**
2. **High voltage supply**
3. **Detector circuit**

Process:

1. Radiation enters the tube.
2. Gas inside becomes **ionized**.
3. This creates an **electrical pulse**.
4. The device counts the pulses.

More pulses → **higher radioactivity**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Hydrometer ❌ (Incorrect)

A **hydrometer** measures the **density or specific gravity of liquids**.

Examples:

• Checking alcohol concentration
• Measuring battery electrolyte density

It has **no relation to radioactivity**.

---

## B. Geiger Counter ✔ (Correct)

A **Geiger counter** detects and measures **radioactive radiation**.

Uses:

• Radiation monitoring
• Nuclear laboratories
• Environmental radiation detection
• Nuclear safety checks

Thus this is the **correct answer**.

---

## C. Seismometer ❌ (Incorrect)

A **seismometer** measures **earthquakes or seismic waves**.

It is used in **geology and earthquake monitoring**, not in nuclear physics.

---

## D. Ammeter ❌ (Incorrect)

An **ammeter** measures **electric current in a circuit**.

Unit measured:

[
\text{Ampere (A)}
]

It does not detect radiation.

---

# Quick Comparison

| Instrument | Measures |
|---|---|
| Geiger Counter | Radioactivity |
| Hydrometer | Liquid density |
| Seismometer | Earthquakes |
| Ammeter | Electric current |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

If the question contains **radioactivity measurement**, the answer is almost always:

👉 **Geiger Counter**

---

You can also review **important scientific instruments and their uses**, which are frequently asked in **DRDO, SSC, and other competitive examinations**. Questions about **scientific instruments and what they measure** appear frequently in the General Science section of **DRDO CEPTAM 11 Tier-1**. These are usually **direct one-line MCQs**.

Below is a **high-yield list to memorize**.

---

# 25 Important Scientific Instruments and Their Uses

| Instrument | What it Measures |
|---|---|
| Thermometer | Temperature |
| Barometer | Atmospheric pressure |
| Hydrometer | Density of liquids |
| Hygrometer | Humidity in air |
| Anemometer | Wind speed |
| Seismometer | Earthquake vibrations |
| Geiger Counter | Radioactivity |
| Ammeter | Electric current |
| Voltmeter | Electric potential difference |
| Galvanometer | Small electric current |
| Calorimeter | Heat energy |
| Lactometer | Purity of milk |
| Altimeter | Altitude (height above sea level) |
| Pyrometer | Very high temperature |
| Manometer | Gas pressure |
| Spectrometer | Spectrum of light |
| Speedometer | Speed of vehicle |
| Odometer | Distance traveled |
| Radar | Detects objects using radio waves |
| Sonar | Detects underwater objects |
| Microscope | Magnifies small objects |
| Telescope | Observes distant objects |
| Potentiometer | Measures potential difference accurately |
| Chronometer | Accurate time measurement |
| Electroscope | Detects electric charge |

---

# Very Important Ones (Most Frequently Asked)

Memorize these first:

1. **Thermometer → Temperature**
2. **Barometer → Atmospheric pressure**
3. **Anemometer → Wind speed**
4. **Seismometer → Earthquakes**
5. **Geiger counter → Radioactivity**
6. **Hydrometer → Density of liquids**
7. **Ammeter → Electric current**
8. **Voltmeter → Voltage**

---

# Example Exam MCQs

### 1. Which instrument is used to measure atmospheric pressure?

A. Thermometer
B. Barometer
C. Hydrometer
D. Hygrometer

✅ **Answer: Barometer**

---

### 2. Wind speed is measured by:

A. Anemometer
B. Hygrometer
C. Thermometer
D. Barometer

✅ **Answer: Anemometer**

---

### 3. Radioactivity is detected by:

A. Ammeter
B. Hydrometer
C. Geiger counter
D. Voltmeter

✅ **Answer: Geiger counter**

---

# 10-Second Memory Trick

Group them:

**Weather instruments**

• Barometer → Pressure
• Hygrometer → Humidity
• Anemometer → Wind speed

**Electricity instruments**

• Ammeter → Current
• Voltmeter → Voltage
• Galvanometer → Small current

**Physics instruments**

• Geiger counter → Radioactivity
